characters.relation: Father
characters.blurb: Athenian of decent but middling house; Themistocles’s hunger for greatness began in that modest shadow.

characters.relation: Persian king at Salamis
characters.blurb: Invaded Greece with a vast host. Themistocles’s messages and timing at Salamis drew the Persian fleet into the narrows.

characters.relation: Rival called the Just
characters.blurb: Ostracized amid Themistocles’s rise, then recalled against Xerxes; at Salamis the old enemies worked as one.

characters.relation: Spartan admiral of the allied fleet
characters.blurb: Nominal commander at Artemisium and Salamis; Themistocles managed him with argument, threats, and bribes.

characters.relation: Spartan regent; later traitor
characters.blurb: Victor of Plataea whose Medism and arrogance later tainted Sparta—and entangled Themistocles in suspicion.

characters.relation: Younger rival; son of Miltiades
characters.blurb: Rose as Themistocles faded; more acceptable to the aristocrats, he inherited Athens’s war against Persia.

characters.relation: Marathon victor; Cimon’s father
characters.blurb: His glory at Marathon was the standard Themistocles burned to surpass by making Athens a sea power.

characters.relation: Persian court figure in the exile tale
characters.blurb: Named in the intrigue around Themistocles’s reception in Asia after his flight from Greece.

characters.relation: Trusted servant; false message to Xerxes
characters.blurb: Pedagogue sent to tell Xerxes the Greeks were trapped and ready to flee—so the King would fight in the straits.

characters.relation: Molossian king who sheltered him
characters.blurb: Former enemy who received Themistocles as a suppliant at the hearth when Athens and Sparta hunted him.

characters.relation: Host on the road to the King
characters.blurb: Aeolian who hid Themistocles and conveyed him inland toward the Persian court in a closed litter.

characters.relation: Rhodesian poet-enemy
characters.blurb: Abusive lyric poet who lampooned Themistocles as corrupt and Medizing—a voice of the hate he left behind.

characters.relation: Poet and friend
characters.blurb: Cean poet who shared Themistocles’s company; Plutarch uses their exchanges to show the general’s wit and vanity.

characters.relation: Daughter
characters.blurb: Named among Themistocles’s children; her marriages mark how his house spread even in exile’s shadow.

characters.relation: Unworthy rival for command
characters.blurb: Ambitious man Themistocles bought off so he would not hold a generalship in the Persian crisis.

characters.relation: Athenian captain at Artemisium
characters.blurb: Commander Themistocles coaxed (and paid) to stay and fight when the Greek fleet wavered.

characters.relation: Persian general left in Greece
characters.blurb: Xerxes’s lieutenant who stayed to finish the land war after Salamis; background to Plataea’s later victory.

characters.relation: Biographical source cited
characters.blurb: Thasian writer Plutarch quotes for anecdotes of Themistocles, Cimon, and Pericles.

characters.relation: Next generation of Athenian power
characters.blurb: Mentioned as the statesman who inherited the naval Athens Themistocles had forced into being.

characters.relation: Syracusan tyrant in an anecdote
characters.blurb: Appears in a story illustrating Themistocles’s sharp tongue among foreign princes.

characters.relation: Source on his end
characters.blurb: Eresian author cited for details of Themistocles’s reception in Persia and death.

characters.relation: Historian of his later reputation
characters.blurb: Praised Themistocles’s foresight—especially the Piraeus—more coolly than comic poets had.

characters.relation: Philosopher who judged him
characters.blurb: Among later writers who weighed Themistocles’s cleverness against Aristides’s virtue.

characters.relation: City of his Persian fief
characters.blurb: Asian Greek city from which Themistocles drew bread, meat, and wine in the King’s grant—gilded exile.

characters.relation: Subject; architect of Salamis
characters.blurb: Athenian of middling birth whose foresight built the Piraeus and the fleet that broke Xerxes at Salamis. Ostracized by the city he saved, he fled to Persia, accepted a satrapy, and died—whether by choice or poison—before he could fight Athens again.

characters.relation: Possible mother; Thracian woman
characters.blurb: One tradition named a Thracian woman called Abrotonon (or Euterpe of Halicarnassus in another) as Themistocles's mother—his obscure birth part of what drove his ferocious ambition.

characters.relation: Historian who praised his foresight
characters.blurb: Gave Themistocles his most famous endorsement—the praise of his ability to see at a glance what a situation required—which Thucydides and Plutarch repeat and refine.

characters.relation: Historian who counted his children
characters.blurb: Chian historian Plutarch cites for a different, larger reckoning of Themistocles's children and marriages—evidence that the general's domestic life was as various as his politics.

characters.relation: Wise adviser before Salamis
characters.blurb: Athenian who told Themistocles, before the allied council could dissolve, that the one thing needed was to fight in the strait—the counsel Themistocles brought to Eurybiades as if it were his own.

characters.relation: Rival general; victor at Mycale
characters.blurb: Led Athenian forces in the destruction of the Persian fleet at Mycale after Salamis; as a political rival, he also once prosecuted Miltiades—a reminder that Athenian gratitude was never lasting.

characters.relation: Xerxes's admiral-brother who fell at Salamis
characters.blurb: Xerxes's bravest brother commanded part of the Persian fleet; his death in the battle of Salamis removed the most formidable of the king's naval commanders.

characters.relation: Historian of Cyzicus cited
characters.blurb: Cited by Plutarch for details about the children of Themistocles, including the daughter's marriages—one of the minor sources that cluster around the exile years.

characters.relation: Goddess of the temple he built and named
characters.blurb: Themistocles built a shrine to Artemis 'of the Best Counsel' in his deme Melite—naming the goddess after his own strategic gift, a typical piece of Themistoclean self-promotion.

characters.relation: Son who recovered his bones for Athens
characters.blurb: Named as the son who eventually brought Themistocles's bones back from Magnesia to be re-buried secretly in Attica, according to the exiled general's own wish.

characters.relation: Son famous for horsemanship
characters.blurb: Among the children of Themistocles, Cleophantus was remembered as a brilliant horseman—a fact Plutarch preserves from Plato's dialogue on teachable virtue.

characters.relation: Goddess whose serpent deserted Athens before Salamis
characters.blurb: The sacred serpent kept in Athena's temple disappeared before the battle—a portent the priests used to urge Athenians to abandon the city. Plutarch also tells the story of Athena and Neptune disputing for Athens.

characters.relation: God whose oracle directed Themistocles in exile
characters.blurb: The oracle of Dodona commanded Themistocles to go to the man who bore a name like Jupiter's own—leading him to Admetus.

characters.relation: Demi-god compared to the low-born achiever
characters.blurb: Plutarch invokes Hercules—himself of mixed divine and mortal blood—as an analogy for Themistocles, who was born outside full civic standing yet surpassed those of pure birth.

characters.relation: Philosopher said to have taught him
characters.blurb: Some sources place Themistocles among Anaxagoras's hearers, though Plutarch notes this is chronologically improbable—Melissus commanded Samos decades after Salamis.

characters.relation: Philosopher said to have taught him
characters.blurb: Eleatic philosopher named alongside Anaxagoras as a possible teacher of Themistocles—an attribution Plutarch doubts on chronological grounds.

characters.relation: Father of Aristides
characters.blurb: Father of Aristides the Just—named by Plutarch to identify the rival whom Themistocles spent his youth contesting.

characters.relation: Beautiful youth both Aristides and Themistocles loved
characters.blurb: Stesilaus of Ceos, whose beauty inflamed both Themistocles and Aristides in youth; Ariston the philosopher cites this shared passion as the boyhood origin of their lifelong rivalry.

characters.relation: Philosopher who reported their rivalry's origin
characters.blurb: Philosopher cited by Plutarch as the source for the story that Themistocles's and Aristides's enmity began over the beautiful Stesilaus of Ceos.

characters.relation: Horse-breeder Themistocles threatened
characters.blurb: Refused Themistocles a colt; was told he would have a wooden horse in his house—meaning a lawsuit among his kin—if he did not comply. A minor story showing Themistocles's combative nature.

characters.relation: Lute-player Themistocles courted for fame
characters.blurb: Epicles of Hermione, a celebrated lute-player, was courted by the young Themistocles to practice at his house—his earliest stratagem for making himself the talk of the city.

characters.relation: Archon when Themistocles sponsored the prize play
characters.blurb: Named on the tablet Themistocles erected to record the dramatic prize: 'Themistocles was at the charge of it; Phrynichus made it; Adimantus was archon'—a permanent monument of his ambition.

characters.relation: Tragic dramatist whose play Themistocles sponsored
characters.blurb: Early Athenian tragic playwright; Themistocles paid for his prize-winning production and had the fact inscribed on a public tablet—his first bid for cultural as well as political fame.

characters.relation: Spartan king killed at Thermopylae
characters.blurb: Fell holding the pass at Thermopylae; news of his death reached Artemisium and prompted the Greek fleet's withdrawal—the moment Themistocles turned the retreat into strategy.

characters.relation: Troezenian who proposed sheltering Athenian refugees
characters.blurb: Proposed the Troezenian decree that housed and fed Athenian families—two obols a day per person, free schooling for children—when Themistocles ordered the evacuation of Athens.

characters.relation: Arcadian leader who helped unite the Greeks
characters.blurb: Arcadian of great assistance to Themistocles in composing Greek civil quarrels before the war with Persia—one of the quiet diplomatic achievements Plutarch credits to Themistocles's energy.

characters.relation: Of Zelea; degraded for bringing Persian gold
characters.blurb: Arthmius of Zelea came carrying Persian money to corrupt Greek resistance; Themistocles had him formally disfranchised with all his descendants—a striking act of pre-war deterrence.

characters.relation: Euboean envoy who bribed Eurybiades through Themistocles
characters.blurb: Sent secretly by the Euboeans with money to persuade Themistocles—and through him Eurybiades—to keep the Greek fleet at Artemisium and not abandon Euboea to Persia.

characters.relation: Spartan king named in Timocreon's poem
characters.blurb: Leotychidas II of Sparta; praised alongside Pausanias and Xanthippus in the bitter poem Timocreon wrote against Themistocles for failing to restore him to his native Rhodes.

characters.relation: Athenian who indicted Themistocles for treason
characters.blurb: Son of Alcmaeon of Agraule; filed the indictment of treason against Themistocles in Athens while the Spartans simultaneously pressed the charge—the alliance of enemies that drove him to flight.

characters.relation: Of Acharnae; saved Themistocles's family, then executed
characters.blurb: Secretly sent Themistocles's wife and children out of Athens to Admetus; Cimon afterward put him to death for it—proof that Athenian gratitude to its greatest general had curdled into persecution.

characters.relation: Wife of Admetus; guided Themistocles's supplication
characters.blurb: Queen of the Molossians who, some say, told Themistocles to take the king's child in his arms and lie at the hearth—the only form of supplication the Molossians would not refuse.

characters.relation: Poet who praised the fight at Artemisium
characters.blurb: Plutarch quotes his verse—'There the sons of Athens set / the stone that freedom stands on yet'—as the most apt praise for the engagement that gave the Greeks confidence to fight at Salamis.

characters.relation: Bounty-hunter who watched for Themistocles at Cyme
characters.blurb: Named alongside Pythodorus among those stationed along the coast to capture Themistocles, with the Persian king's two-hundred-talent reward making the hunt well worth running.

characters.relation: Bounty-hunter at Cyme alongside Ergoteles
characters.blurb: Staked out the coast with Ergoteles to catch Themistocles; the fugitive fled to Nicogenes's house at Aegae to hide while the two men combed the shore.

characters.relation: Attendant who prophesied in Nicogenes's household
characters.blurb: Child-attendant in Nicogenes's house who fell into a prophetic frenzy the night before Themistocles's secret departure and cried in verse: 'Night shall speak, and night instruct thee.'

characters.relation: Exiled Spartan king at the Persian court
characters.blurb: Asked by the king for any gift he chose; requested to ride in state through Sardis with the royal tiara, which Mithropaustes and the king angrily refused. Themistocles interceded to calm the king's anger.

characters.relation: King's cousin who rebuked Demaratus
characters.blurb: Touched Demaratus on the head and told him he had no brains for the royal tiara to cover—even if Jupiter gave him his thunder, he would not be Jupiter for that.

characters.relation: Persian governor who tried to assassinate Themistocles
characters.blurb: Governor of upper Phrygia who hired Pisidians to ambush Themistocles at Lion's-head; a dream warned him to take the long road, and the assassins mistook his wet tent-cloth for the man himself.

characters.relation: Wife of Themistocles; daughter of Lysander of Alopece
characters.blurb: Mother of Archeptolis, Polyeuctus, and Cleophantus by Themistocles; her father Lysander of Alopece later adopted their son Diocles.

characters.relation: Father of Archippe; adopted Themistocles's son Diocles
characters.blurb: Distinct from the Spartan admiral Lysander: an Athenian of the deme Alopece whose daughter Archippe married Themistocles, and who raised their son Diocles after the exile.

characters.relation: Eldest surviving son of Themistocles by Archippe
characters.blurb: First of three sons born to Archippe; married his half-sister Mnesiptolema after their father's death—a union across the household that Plutarch records as a family curiosity.

characters.relation: Son of Themistocles by Archippe
characters.blurb: Second of the three sons Themistocles had by Archippe; named in the family register Plutarch and Theopompus preserve, but otherwise unrecorded.

characters.relation: Son of Themistocles; adopted by his grandfather
characters.blurb: One of Themistocles's older sons; adopted by his maternal grandfather Lysander of Alopece after Themistocles's fall—the family network adjusting to the disaster of exile.

characters.relation: Son of Themistocles; died in boyhood
characters.blurb: Named after his grandfather; one of Themistocles's older sons who died young from a horse-bite—a sad irony that the great strategist's son fell to the creature his daughter-in-law's father bred.

characters.relation: Nephew who married Themistocles's daughter
characters.blurb: After Themistocles's death, went to Magnesia and married his daughter Nicomache, and took charge of the youngest daughter Asia—a kinsman who gathered the family's remnants in exile.

characters.relation: Comic poet (distinct from the philosopher)
characters.blurb: Athenian comedian who confirmed, in verse, the tradition that Themistocles's tomb stood near the harbor of Piraeus—Plutarch cites him as a source alongside Diodorus the cosmographer.

characters.relation: Historian who invented sons for Themistocles
characters.blurb: Wrote a melodramatic history that brought in Neocles and Demopolis as Themistocles's sons to stir tragic pathos; Plutarch rejects this as invention and calls it a stage machine.

characters.relation: Cosmographer who placed Themistocles's tomb near Piraeus
characters.blurb: The cosmographer-geographer cited for the conjecture that Themistocles's tomb lay on the promontory of Alcimus near the harbor—supported, Plutarch adds, by Plato the comedian's verse.

characters.relation: Philosopher in whose house the later Themistocles lived
characters.blurb: Philosopher in whose house Plutarch himself had an intimate acquaintance with another Themistocles of Athens—a descendant who still enjoyed honors from Magnesia in Plutarch's own day.

characters.relation: Historian who held that Xerxes was already dead
characters.blurb: Local historian of Lampsacus who, with Thucydides, reported that Themistocles arrived at the Persian court after Xerxes was dead—a view the chronological tables support, though Ephorus disagrees.

characters.relation: Historian who placed Themistocles at Xerxes's court
characters.blurb: Listed by Plutarch (as Heraclides) among those who say Themistocles came to Xerxes rather than to his successor—part of the ancient disagreement about the Persian exile chronology.

characters.relation: Historian who held that Themistocles met Xerxes
characters.blurb: Persian historian cited by Plutarch alongside Ephorus, Clitarchus, and Heraclides for placing Themistocles's arrival in Persia during Xerxes's reign rather than his son's.

characters.relation: Historian who placed Themistocles at Xerxes's court
characters.blurb: Author of a life of Alexander and various histories; cited here among those who say Themistocles met Xerxes in person rather than arriving after his death.

characters.relation: Attic historian who credited Themistocles for the fleet's manning
characters.blurb: Atthidographer who attributed the eight-drachma payment that manned the fleet at Salamis to Themistocles's ingenuity—a detail Aristotle credited instead to the Areopagus.

characters.relation: Daughter of Themistocles; married to Panthoides
characters.blurb: Named among Themistocles's daughters; married Panthoides of the island of Chios—one of the family's dispersal across the Greek world after Themistocles's fall.

characters.relation: Daughter of Themistocles; married to Nicomedes
characters.blurb: Named among Themistocles's daughters; married Nicomedes the Athenian—part of the domestic record Theopompus and Neanthes preserve where Plutarch counts the general's many children.

characters.relation: Daughter of Themistocles; married her nephew Phrasicles
characters.blurb: After her father's death her cousin Phrasicles came from Athens to Magnesia and married her, with her brothers' consent, and took charge of the youngest daughter Asia.

characters.relation: Athenian who married Sybaris
characters.blurb: Athenian husband of Sybaris, Themistocles's daughter; named among the family connections that spread out from the exiled general's household.

characters.relation: Chian who married Italia
characters.blurb: From the island of Chios; married Italia, one of Themistocles's daughters—one of the marriages through which the family of the exile settled across the Aegean.

characters.relation: Father of Leobotes the accuser
characters.blurb: Of Agraule; his son Leobotes filed the treason indictment against Themistocles in Athens while the Spartans pressed the same charge simultaneously—the coordinated assault that forced Themistocles into permanent exile.

characters.relation: Athenian captain who helped kill Ariamenes at Salamis
characters.blurb: Aminias the Decelean, sailing in the same vessel as Sosicles the Pedian, rammed his ship into the Persian flagship; the two vessels locked together, and Ariamenes, Xerxes's admiral, was killed in the fight that followed.

characters.relation: Athenian captain at Salamis; Aminias's shipmate
characters.blurb: Sosicles the Pedian sailed with Aminias the Decelean; when their ship locked beaks with Ariamenes's Persian flagship, the two fought their way aboard and killed the Persian admiral in close combat.

characters.relation: Persian eunuch used as Themistocles's secret messenger to Xerxes
characters.blurb: Captured eunuch of the Persian king whom Themistocles sent after Salamis with the false message that the Greeks planned to sail north and destroy the bridge—a stratagem to hurry Xerxes's retreat and keep credit for restraining the Greeks.

characters.relation: Tragedian; eyewitness source for the Persian ship count
characters.blurb: Fought at Marathon and Salamis; Plutarch quotes lines from his Persians as 'certain knowledge' giving the exact count of the Persian fleet—the most famous contemporary account of the battle Themistocles won.

characters.relation: Historian who placed Xerxes's observation post at Megara
characters.blurb: Placed Xerxes's golden throne not above the temple of Hercules on the Attic shore (as Phanodemus said) but on the hills called the Horns in Megara—one of the minor disagreements among ancient sources that Plutarch records without adjudicating.

characters.relation: Atthidographer who placed Xerxes at the Attic promontory
characters.blurb: Attic local historian who said Xerxes watched Salamis from the promontory above the temple of Hercules on the Attic coast; Acestodorus put the throne at the Horns in Megara—Plutarch presents both without deciding.

characters.relation: Father of Epicydes the demagogue
characters.blurb: Father of Epicydes, the popular speaker of eloquent tongue but fearful heart who tried to seize the command from Themistocles before the crisis; Plutarch identifies him by his father's name in the standard Athenian manner.

characters.relation: Seer who demanded the three Persian captives be sacrificed
characters.blurb: The fleet prophet who, seeing fire blaze from the offerings and a man sneeze on the right, seized Themistocles's hand and demanded the three richly-dressed Persian prisoners be consecrated and sacrificed to Bacchus the Devourer—a terrible omen that the crowd, in its terror, enforced.

characters.relation: Persian whose children were sacrificed before Salamis
characters.blurb: Three richly clothed young men described as children of Artayctes and Sandauce (Xerxes's sister) were brought before Themistocles as he was about to sacrifice; the prophet Euphrantides demanded they be offered up to Bacchus, and the terrified crowd assented.

characters.relation: Sister of Xerxes; mother of the sacrificed captives
characters.blurb: Sister of Xerxes; the three beautiful, richly dressed captives offered as human sacrifice before Salamis were said to be her children by Artayctes—a detail that heightened the terrible portent of the omen.

characters.relation: Scholar who added detail about Themistocles's secret contact
characters.blurb: In his treatise on Riches, Eratosthenes records that Themistocles's message to Artabanus (via Arnaces) was facilitated by a woman of Eretria kept by Artabanus—a further strand of the back-channel diplomacy that eased Themistocles's reception at the Persian court.

nameResolutions.note: Son Neocles who died young of a horse-bite
Phylarchus's fictional reference to Neocles as a son
